topic: A person's manuscript is rejected by their dream publisher
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed on my screen like a physical blow. "Regretfully, we will not be moving forward with your manuscript." My hands began to tremble so violently I had to grip the edge of the desk to keep them from flying across the keyboard. A strange, hot pressure built behind my eyes, making the fluorescent lights of my tiny apartment seem to pulse. I reread the rejection letter, the words blurring into meaningless shapes. I stood up, pacing the cramped space, my breath coming in short, shallow gasps. I slammed a fist onto the worn wooden surface, the sound echoing too loudly in the silence. It felt like the world had tilted on its axis. Why? What had I done wrong? My throat tightened, a desperate need to scream clawing at my vocal cords, but no sound emerged, only a choked, ragged exhale.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, my vision tunneling until the rejection notice was the only thing sharp in the room. A sudden, dizzying wave washed over me, and I stumbled backward, knocking over a stack of annotated drafts. The paper scattered across the floor, a chaotic mess mirroring the state of my mind. I dropped to my knees, clawing at the scattered pages, my fingers frantic, searching for some hidden flaw, some glaring oversight that could justify this utter dismissal. A low, involuntary sound escaped my chest—a strangled, high-pitched noise that felt alien and terrifying. I pressed my palms against my temples, trying to physically contain the swirling chaos inside my head, the feeling of being utterly unmoored and exposed overwhelming.

[story 3]

The phone call ended, and I didn't hang up. I just sat there, staring at the dead receiver, the silence of the room amplifying the frantic drumming in my chest. Every nerve ending felt stretched to breaking point. I began to pace the length of my living room, a tight, jerky motion that seemed to consume all available space. My jaw was clenched so tightly I could feel the muscles in my neck strain. I kept running my hands through my hair, pulling at strands as if trying to unravel the very knot of disappointment tightening in my stomach. I walked to the window, gripping the sill until my knuckles were white, watching the indifferent city lights below, feeling a profound, sharp sense of injustice bubble up, threatening to spill over.
